alicia-payne-canberra.summary: In a 28 July 2025 interview, Payne said she has continually called for an immediate and permanent ceasefire in Gaza as the most effective way to protect innocent lives, stated that by not allowing aid into Gaza Israel is breaching international law, and said she longs to see Australia recognise a Palestinian state as soon as possible, at a time most advantageous to peace.

andrew-leigh-fenner.summary: Leigh has advocated for evidence-based foreign aid, stating the Australian Government understands its obligation to ensure aid delivered has the maximum positive impact and arguing that randomised trials and rigorous evaluation should determine which development interventions work.

david-smith-bean.summary: Smith is listed as an Australian member of the Inter-Parliamentary Alliance on China (IPAC), a cross-party international group of legislators focused on relations with the People's Republic of China.

sources.title: David Smith MP - IPAC The Alliance

alex-hawke-mitchell.summary: As Minister for International Development and the Pacific, Hawke called for closer ties to and development assistance for the Pacific region, stating 'their prosperity is our prosperity,' while supporting a reduction in the overall foreign aid budget. He also argued his Pacific engagement did not require further action by Australia on climate change.

allegra-spender-wentworth.summary: Spender signed a joint letter with other crossbench (teal) MPs requesting that Foreign Minister Penny Wong restore Australian funding to the UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestinian Refugees (UNRWA).

angus-taylor-hume.summary: As Shadow Defence Minister, Taylor said on 15 July 2025 that Australia should raise its defence budget from around 2.3% of GDP to at least 3%, that this is vital to support commitments under the AUKUS pact, and that Australia must be prepared to act on Taiwan's security with clear, principled commitments from Canberra.

sources.title: Australia Must Be Ready to Support Taiwan, Says Angus Taylor

anthony-albanese-grayndler.summary: As Prime Minister, Albanese's government formally recognised the State of Palestine (announced August 2025, effective 21 September 2025) as part of a coordinated push for a two-state solution; he officially commenced the AUKUS security pact on 13 March 2023 (acquiring nuclear-powered submarines); restored high-level dialogue with China, meeting Xi Jinping in November 2022 and visiting China in November 2023; and pledged a further $100 million in aid to Ukraine in July 2022.

barnaby-joyce-new-england.summary: On 10 August 2018, Joyce defended maintaining Australia's foreign aid budget, framing aid to Pacific island nations as a strategic necessity, saying Australia needs to support island nations 'otherwise the Chinese government will just move in there and support them.'

sources.title: Joyce defends foreign aid against farmers' criticism

chris-bowen-mcmahon.summary: In an ABC TV Insiders interview on 26 November 2023, Bowen said 'Israel can only really be secure and free when Palestine is secure and free, and ultimately, that is our long term objective,' reaffirmed that Israel has rights and obligations under international law, and said the government wanted ceasefire arrangements to become more permanent as the pathway to peace.

sources.title: Chris Bowen: Israel can only be free when Palestine is

ed-husic-chifley.summary: Husic supports recognition of a Palestinian state and, after being dropped from cabinet, said on 12 May 2025 the government should do whatever it could to back Palestinian statehood as quickly as possible; he described mass starvation in Gaza as a conscious decision and the use of starvation as a war crime, and in May 2025 called for targeted sanctions against Israeli government ministers overseeing the military campaign in Gaza.

sources.title: Dumped cabinet minister Ed Husic urges Labor to push for Palestinian statehood

jason-clare-blaxland.summary: On 10 October 2024, Clare said that while every country has a right to defend itself it must comply with international law, and when asked whether Israel was complying he said 'the bombing of schools and hospitals, I think, are not complying with international law,' adding that his constituents were asking for a ceasefire and for the war to end.

sources.title: Labor minister Jason Clare suggests Israel 'not complying with international law'

jerome-laxale-bennelong.summary: In a 2022 parliamentary speech on the Iran protests following the death of Mahsa Amini in morality-police custody, Laxale said he stood for his local Iranian community in Bennelong and used his position to ensure their message and that of their families was heard.

sources.title: Speeches - Human Rights - The 2022 Iran Protests

julian-leeser-berowra.summary: Leeser supports Australian recognition of West Jerusalem as Israel's capital and called the Albanese government's 2022 reversal 'shambolic'. He opposes an immediate Gaza ceasefire while hostages are held and Hamas remains, saying a ceasefire would let terrorist organisations 'regroup and reform', and opposes imposing a timetable on a two-state solution 'without full and proper negotiations on the final-status issues'. After the October 2023 Hamas attack he called on Australia to suspend diplomatic relations with Iran.

sources.title: Speech to the House of Representatives - First Anniversary of the Hamas Terror Attacks on Israel

matt-thistlethwaite-kingsford-smith.summary: In a House of Representatives debate (2 June 2026) Thistlethwaite presented the government's supportive position on AUKUS, stating that nuclear-propelled submarines 'have a longer range and endurance and greater stealth, which means that they can go undetected for longer' than conventional alternatives, and framing AUKUS and the three-phase submarine acquisition plan as essential to Australia's security through strong partnerships and alliances.

michael-mccormack-riverina.summary: Responding to a protest outside his Wagga office, McCormack stated that protest organisers 'do not represent the majority of the Riverina who support Israel', and added: 'Palestine has been recognised as a state by the Australian government and the people there do need to live in peace.'

sources.title: 'They don't represent the Riverina': MP slams protest against Israeli president

mike-freelander-macarthur.summary: In a parliamentary statement (16 October 2023) Freelander affirmed 'Israel has a right to exist ... and Israel has the right to protect itself' and condemned Hamas, saying it had 'opened the gates of hell'. He also grieved for civilian casualties on all sides, stating 'in this conflict the Palestinians will be victims just as much as the Israelis', and expressed hope for the safe release of hostages and a resolution with minimal further loss of life.

nicolette-boele-bradfield.summary: Boele has called twice for a parliamentary inquiry into the AUKUS deal. In a June 2025 interview she said there is 'a muddiness ... between the proposed AUKUS plan and what it's trying to achieve' and that 'if it's deterrence, putting all our eggs into this basket doesn't seem ... a really good strategy', describing an inquiry as a prudent investment.

sources.title: Retooling our economy: Nicolette Boele on AUKUS, environmental law, and the future of Australia

pat-conroy-shortland.summary: As Minister for Defence Industry and Pacific Island Affairs, Conroy stated (December 2024) that 'AUKUS is not about charity. AUKUS is about increasing the collective security of the three nations involved' and that it would endure because it is in all three countries' self-interest. On China he emphasised arguing 'for that rules-based order', and on the Pacific said Australia will demonstrate it 'will not enter into security or policing agreements with a third country without agreement by Australia', citing the Nauru treaty framework.

sources.title: Pat Conroy on defending Australia and Pacific partnerships

sharon-claydon-newcastle.summary: As co-chair of the Parliamentary Friends of Palestine, Claydon co-signed a statement (25 October 2023) calling for 'an immediate ceasefire and an end to the violence in Israel and Palestine', condemning both Hamas attacks and Israeli attacks on Gaza civilians as breaches of humanitarian law, stating 'the protection of civilian lives is paramount and international humanitarian law must be observed', and affirming that peace 'rests on a two-state solution'.

sources.title: Statement by the Parliamentary Friends of Palestine

sophie-scamps-mackellar.summary: In February 2024 Scamps called for the Australian government to restore UNRWA funding and for an immediate ceasefire in the Gaza war. In October 2024 she voted for a resolution condemning the Hamas-led attack on Israel and supported a Greens amendment condemning 'war crimes perpetrated by the State of Israel'. She also targeted an AUKUS adviser appointment in her 2023 'Ending Jobs for Mates Bill' and in February 2023 called for federal recognition of the Armenian, Assyrian and Greek genocides.

susan-templeman-macquarie.summary: Speaking at Labor's national conference (18 August 2023), Templeman supported prioritising recognition of Palestine, saying 'the extreme right-wing policies of the Netanyahu government that speed up the expansion of settlements are a serious impediment to the two-state solution that we are all committed to', and backed 'the call our platform makes for the recognition of Palestine as an issue of priority'.

sources.title: Labor stays the course over Palestine recognition

sussan-ley-farrer.summary: Ley publicly supported admission of the State of Palestine to the UN in 2011 but said her view changed after a 2022 visit to Israel; she reaffirmed the Coalition's commitment to West Jerusalem as Israel's capital. As Opposition Leader in August 2025 she stated she would reverse the Albanese government's decision to recognise a Palestinian state should she win office, saying recognition must come only at the end of a genuine two-state process.

tanya-plibersek-sydney.summary: In 2002 Plibersek described Israel as 'a rogue state' and Ariel Sharon as a 'war criminal', though she later developed friendly relations with the Jewish community and visited Israel and Palestine in 2014. As Shadow Foreign Minister in 2016 she advocated redrawing Australia's maritime border with East Timor. She opposed the 2003 invasion of Iraq and presented a letter signed by 43 Labor MPs opposing invasion without UN approval, and has opposed foreign aid cuts.

tony-burke-watson.summary: Responding to Israeli PM Netanyahu (20 August 2025, ABC Radio National), Home Affairs Minister Burke said 'strength is not measured by how many people you can blow up or how many children you can leave hungry', defended the Albanese government's recognition of Palestinian statehood as a principled decision made after consulting Netanyahu, and stated 'what is happening in Gaza is beyond belief'.

sources.title: Australia hits back at Netanyahu amid escalating diplomatic row over Gaza

zali-steggall-warringah.summary: In August 2024 Steggall accused Liberal leader Peter Dutton of racism over his support for prohibiting Gazans from obtaining Australian visas amid the Gaza war.

luke-john-gosling-solomon.summary: In his Lowy Institute paper 'Deterring at a distance: The strategic logic of AUKUS' (24 June 2024), Gosling argued that 'China's military build-up is the largest and most ambitious of any country since the end of the Second World War', that nuclear-powered submarines 'are optimal deterrence machines' and a 'goldilocks long-distance capability', and defended AUKUS as giving Australia 'added strategic weight to deter a more powerful adversary'.

sources.title: Deterring at a distance: The strategic logic of AUKUS

andrew-wallace-fisher.summary: Wallace is a strong supporter of Israel and serves as Oceania Chair of the Israel Allies Foundation Caucus; he supports Israel's military response to Hamas after the 2023 attack and has urged action against antisemitism in Australia. He urged Australia to provide military support to Ukraine and, as Speaker, arranged for President Zelensky to address Parliament (31 March 2022). He has argued Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps must be kept "weak, broke and totally under control" and sought to minimise Chinese access to sensitive Australian military sites.

jim-chalmers-rankin.summary: On 31 July 2025, Treasurer Jim Chalmers said Australian recognition of a Palestinian state is a matter of "when, not if," framing Palestinian statehood as an important step toward a two-state solution and an end to the "cycle of violence," while conditioning support on Hamas having no role in future Palestinian leadership.

sources.title: After Canada's move, Chalmers says Australian recognition of Palestine about 'when, not if'

angie-bell-moncrieff.summary: In an August 2025 interview, Bell criticised the Albanese Government's move to recognise Palestine, saying the Prime Minister had "decided to put the cart before the horse, before a proper peace process." She said she wants to see the war end, the hostages returned, and a proper peace process before recognition.

sources.title: Transcript – Paul Murray Live, Sky News

milton-dick-oxley.summary: Dick has been described by the Australian Jewish News as a strong supporter of Israel; after a 2017 sponsored visit to Israel he characterised terms such as "settlements, occupation, apartheid and the wall" as "easy catchphrases and cliches" that often lacked counterbalance.

phillip-thompson-herbert.summary: In his maiden speech on 4 July 2019, Thompson, a former infantry soldier, said that as a member of the Joint Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade he would spotlight veterans' issues, and he expressed support for the Australia-Singapore Military Training Initiative as creating jobs and economic growth.

leon-rebello-mcpherson.summary: In his maiden speech on 30 July 2025, Rebello called for Australia to invest in "a strong, sovereign defence capability," to seize the strategic and diplomatic advantages of trusted allies and the Commonwealth, and to diversify import and export markets "so we don't rely on any one country" and avoid being "held ransom by adversaries."

elizabeth-watson-brown-ryan.summary: Per Australian Greens platform: the party (of which Watson-Brown is the MP for Ryan) calls to cancel the AUKUS agreement and renegotiate ANZUS to reduce reliance on the United States, to stop all Australian military exports to Israel, and to end the occupation of Palestinian territories occupied since 1967.

ted-o-brien-fairfax.summary: In September 2020, O'Brien called for Australia to negotiate a free-trade agreement with Taiwan, citing Australia's existing agreements with New Zealand and Singapore. He has also publicly linked the AUKUS agreement to the case for adopting nuclear technology, stating that adopting nuclear-propelled submarines under AUKUS takes Australia's engagement with nuclear technology to a new level.

andrew-wilkie-clark.summary: Wilkie advocates a more independent Australian foreign policy underpinned by adherence to international law, supports increased overseas development assistance, and calls for Russian withdrawal from Ukraine. On Israel/Palestine he states Israel has been engaged in a 'decades-long, systematic campaign of occupation and oppression in Palestine' and that 'peace can only come with formal recognition of Palestine by all parties and a two-state solution,' calling for an end to Israel's campaign in Gaza and the West Bank. He argues Australia would be better served by a more independent security posture 'not beholden to' partnerships like ANZUS, the Quad and AUKUS.

tony-zappia-makin.summary: Zappia has stated in Parliament that he acknowledges the Palestinian people's right to self-determination, including the need to recognise a sovereign and independent State of Palestine, and the need to address the root causes of the conflict including dismantling Israel's 'system of apartheid against all Palestinians.' In an October 2023 speech he said 'the killing of innocent Palestinians in Gaza must also stop' and that 'Israel must show restraint and respect for international law,' and he has called for a permanent two-state resolution.

basem-abdo-calwell.summary: Abdo, the first person of Palestinian Arab descent in the Australian Parliament, has stated 'My position on this is very clear. I support the recognition of a Palestinian state,' framing it as the right to self-determination rather than symbolism. Drawing on a 2011 visit to the West Bank, he described being confronted by 'how overarching the occupation is, how it impacts the daily lives in all aspects for the Palestinian people there,' and said party-room discussions on timing would be held in caucus.

sources.title: New Labor MP Basem Abdo reveals how he'll champion Palestinian statehood

richard-marles-corio.summary: As Defence Minister, Marles is a strong supporter of AUKUS, saying it "is going to really drive the biggest uplift in the Australian defense industry that we've seen" and that the partnership requires contributing to the US submarine industrial base. On China he describes recent incidents as "unsafe and unprofessional" but seeks stabilisation, saying the nations should "cooperate where we can, disagree where we must" and advocates resuming defence dialogue to reduce miscalculation.

sources.title: EXCLUSIVE: Australian defense minister Richard Marles on AUKUS, China and industry concerns

josh-burns-macnamara.summary: Burns, a Jewish Labor MP, supports a two-state solution and says he "desperately want[s] to see a peace agreement signed between the Israelis and the Palestinians." Following the 7 October 2023 attack he opposed an immediate ceasefire, arguing it would allow Hamas to regroup, and he opposed the government's vote in favour of UN General Assembly Resolution ES-10/22 on Gaza. In October 2022 he criticised the Albanese government for revoking Australia's recognition of Jerusalem as Israel's capital.

mark-dreyfus-isaacs.summary: In a parliamentary motion condemning Hamas, Dreyfus said "Australia unambiguously supports the right of Israel to defend itself" and that "the Australian Government stands as one with Israel and the people of Israel against Hamas," while also stating the government "will continue to support the legitimate rights and aspirations of the Palestinian people" and endorsing "a just and enduring two state solution – in which Israel and a future Palestinian state coexist in peace and security." He described Hamas as having "no interest in making peace with Israel."

sources.title: Motion condemning Hamas attacks on Israel - Mark Dreyfus KC MP

peter-khalil-wills.summary: Per his Wikipedia article, Khalil "is a strong supporter of the Western alliance" and a "very strong supporter of the US alliance," and on China co-authored a statement that "Bowing to Beijing would be the modern equivalent of the Munich Agreement," advocating "containment of Chinese totalitarian threats to democratic nations." He serves as Assistant Minister for Defence.

ged-kearney-cooper.summary: In a Federation Chamber speech, Kearney condemned Hamas's actions as "evil" while stating "the answer to the senseless killings of Hamas cannot be the collective punishment of the Palestinian people," calling for Israel "to operate by the rules of international law" and referencing the UN call for "a suspension of hostilities... without any delay." She affirmed the "legitimate aspirations for the Palestinian people" and support for "a just and enduring peace, a two-state solution."

sources.title: SPEECH (Federation Chamber): Tragedies in Gaza and Israel

kate-thwaites-jagajaga.summary: In a February 2024 parliamentary speech, Thwaites said she shared "the horror... at the destruction and death that have been occurring in Gaza under Israel's military action" and expressed devastation at Israel's planned Rafah ground offensive. She noted "Australia has voted for an immediate humanitarian ceasefire," said any ceasefire "cannot be a one-sided ceasefire" and "will also require Hamas to cease its attacks and to release the Israeli hostages," and said Australia expects Israel to act in accordance with the ICJ ruling.

julian-hill-bruce.summary: In a July 2024 statement, Hill said "Israel's Knesset vote against a Palestinian State is profoundly disappointing" and that "a two-state solution is the only realistic resolution to decades of violence," describing alternatives (one state) as "fantasies" or (apartheid or genocide) as "horrific." He called the acceleration of illegal West Bank settlements "appalling" and said major countries need to "collectively intervene... to impose consequences on Israel" to stop them.

sources.title: Julian Hill MP — Facebook statement on Knesset vote against a Palestinian State

monique-ryan-kooyong.summary: Per her Wikipedia article, Ryan voted for a parliamentary resolution condemning Hamas's October 2023 attack on Israel and voted against a Greens amendment that would also have condemned "war crimes perpetrated by the State of Israel." She called for a "humanitarian pause" in the conflict, describing the "human suffering in Gaza" as "intolerable."

tim-watts-gellibrand.summary: Watts is described as an outspoken advocate of Australian aid in Parliament and a proponent of greater Australian engagement in Asia. He has stated that there is no other nation in Asia more important to Australia's future than Indonesia.

tim-wilson-goldstein.summary: Wilson is a member of the 'Wolverines', a parliamentary group critical of the Chinese government, and has accused Huawei of creating surveillance systems for the Chinese government, urging the UK to ban the company. In an October 2024 advertisement in the Australian Jewish News he described himself as 'proudly Zionist'.

andrew-hastie-canning.summary: Hastie is a prominent critic of Chinese government influence in Australia, having written that 'Communist China' rather than Islamic terrorism would be the central security concern of the 21st century, and supported banning Huawei from Australia's 5G network. In May 2020 he signed a letter condemning what it called an assault on Hong Kong's autonomy, rule of law and fundamental freedoms.

anne-aly-cowan.summary: On recognition of Palestinian statehood, Aly stated that 'just saying to recognize Palestinian state without any context is in some ways tokenistic', supporting the government position that recognition should be part of a just peace process. She is also noted as a researcher on countering violent extremism, having founded People Against Violent Extremism (PaVE).

josh-wilson-fremantle.summary: In a statement dated 8 February 2024, Wilson said of Israel's military campaign in Gaza that 'not every military action constitutes self defence. The wholesale destruction of Gaza is not self defence', and called for the violence to end, describing the killing of civilians as wrong.

sources.title: The violence in Gaza must end

kate-chaney-curtin.summary: Chaney has called for greater scrutiny and transparency of the AUKUS agreement, joining other crossbench MPs in pressing for more parliamentary accountability over the deal.

sources.title: Peter Garrett to head up independent inquiry into AUKUS as ranks break in caucus

david-pocock-act.summary: In remarks reported on 3 June 2024, Pocock called on Australia to cut all military ties with Israel and end arms deals, citing Australian-made parts used in F-35 aircraft and an Elbit Systems vehicle contract, and called for targeted sanctions against members of the Israeli government and Israel Defense Forces over the situation in Gaza.

sources.title: Lawmaker urges Australian gov't to end arms deals with Israel

dave-sharma-nsw.summary: Sharma, a former Australian ambassador to Israel, has advocated that Australia spend more than 2% of GDP on defence. On Israel-Gaza he has supported demilitarisation of the Gaza Strip and stated that the conflict will not end until Hamas stops firing rockets.

david-shoebridge-nsw.summary: Shoebridge has been a vocal supporter of Palestinian causes. In August 2025 he said that if the Albanese government stopped the export of F-35 fighter jet parts to Israel, Israel's F-35 fleet would be grounded, and in September 2025 he called for Australia to apply sanctions against Israel comparable to those against Russia.

deborah-o-neill-nsw.summary: O'Neill is described as an 'AUKUS champion' and chairs the Parliamentary Joint Committee on Defence overseeing the AUKUS nuclear-powered submarine program. She is characterised as hawkish on China, is a member of the Inter-Parliamentary Alliance on China, and has stated that Xi Jinping is 'forever tied' to the persecution of Uighurs.

mehreen-faruqi-nsw.summary: Faruqi has been a prominent parliamentary critic of Israel over the Gaza war. In November 2023 she led Greens colleagues in a Senate walkout protesting the government's refusal to call for a ceasefire, and in 2025 she held signs in the Senate reading "Gaza is starving... Sanction Israel" and directly asked PM Albanese, "will you sanction Israel?" She has called for an end to Australian military exports to Israel.

sources.title: Faruqi stages Senate protest, asks Albanese: 'Will you sanction Israel?'

jacinta-nampijinpa-price-nt.summary: Price has publicly criticised the Albanese Government's foreign policy on the Middle East. In an October 2024 opinion piece she defended Israel as "our ally" and accused critics of being silent on Hamas atrocities while accusing Israel of "genocide" for responding. She characterised the government's September 2025 decision to recognise a Palestinian state as "ill-timed, impulsive, irrational, immoral and ideologically-driven".

sources.title: Jacinta Price: Payman's attempt to falsely equate Indigenous disadvantage with Palestinian cause is offensive

malcolm-roberts-qld.summary: Roberts argues Australia should stay out of overseas conflicts, writing on Ukraine that "we have no dog in this fight and we should stay out of it" and questioning the mainstream media narrative on the war. He has called for Australia to avoid being drawn into foreign wars on false premises, citing the Iraq WMD claims.

sources.title: Ukraine: let's take a different perspective

larissa-waters-qld.summary: Waters has criticised AUKUS as wasteful, saying "We are wasting A$370 billion on nuclear submarines that actually may never even eventuate" and that the expenditure "makes us less safe". On the Middle East she has said Australia should play a stronger role in sanctioning Benjamin Netanyahu's war cabinet.

sources.title: Politics with Michelle Grattan: Larissa Waters on why we deserve more than a government that just tinkers

matthew-canavan-qld.summary: Canavan argues Australia's defence strategy has put too much stake on nuclear submarines that would not be delivered until the 2040s, and that the AUKUS agreement has been put under review by the Trump administration. He calls for higher defence spending, warns that "economic dependence on China only deepens their malign influence", and stresses retaining sovereignty by working with close allies like the US.

sources.title: Courier Mail: Wasting billions on net zero when our very security is at stake

pauline-hanson-qld.summary: Hanson has said foreign aid should be judged by whether "it's spent well" and that Australia should "clean up our own backyard first"; One Nation's platform calls for a large cut to the aid budget. She has raised concern about aid-recipient nations also accepting Chinese aid. On the Middle East she has called for Palestinians on temporary visas to be repatriated now the Gaza conflict has ended, saying "charity begins at home".

sources.title: Pauline Hanson has raised an uncomfortable question about Australia's foreign aid. Here's why it matters
Pauline Hanson calls on Albanese to return Palestinian refugees

barbara-pocock-sa.summary: Pocock has criticised the conduct of the Israeli state and military in Gaza, saying "We have certainly been critical of the behaviour of the Israeli state, their military, and the way they continue to conduct a war against the civilians in Gaza." She has also voiced South Australian concerns about AUKUS and called for Australia to forge a more independent foreign policy.

sources.title: Politics with Michelle Grattan: Barbara Pocock on the Greens' policy priorities

paul-scarr-qld.summary: Scarr has stated he supports "a two-state solution that is negotiated between the parties and would provide both Israelis and Palestinians with peace and security within internationally recognized borders", arguing the issue can only be resolved by negotiation between the parties.

sources.title: Politicians Face Israel Scrutiny As Slaughter Continues (Ipswich Tribune)
sources.publisher: Senator Paul Scarr (official website) / Ipswich Tribune

jessica-collins-nsw.summary: In her first Senate speech, Collins advocated shifting Australia's Pacific relationship "from aid and dependency to trade and prosperity", noting Pacific countries "want to move beyond the aid relationship with Australia". She highlighted that remittances from Pacific workers exceed Australian government aid, criticised high transfer fees, and called for stronger economic sanctions on Burma's central bank.

sources.title: Collins, Senator Jessica: 30 Jul 2025: Senate debates (first speech)

penny-wong-sa.summary: As Foreign Minister, Wong said in April 2024 that Australia would consider recognising Palestinian statehood as a means of facilitating a two-state solution, but clarified the May 2024 UN vote on Palestinian participation did not constitute recognition, which would come only "when we think the time is right." She described Israel's planned Rafah offensive as "unjustifiable" (February 2024), paused UNRWA funding in February 2024 pending Israeli evidence, and in June 2025 announced Australia would sanction Israeli ministers Ben-Gvir and Smotrich. She sought to stabilise relations with China, becoming the first Australian cabinet minister to visit China since 2019 (December 2022).

james-paterson-vic.summary: Paterson is a strong critic of China; he and Andrew Hastie were denied entry to China in 2019 over their criticism of Beijing's treatment of Uyghurs, and in 2022 he became Australian Co-Chair of the Inter-Parliamentary Alliance on China. He has stated that China's "foreign interference and espionage are the greatest threat to Australia's way of life, surpassing terrorism" (October 2021). He has advocated a CANZUK freedom-of-movement arrangement between Canada, Australia, New Zealand and the UK.

sources.title: China biggest threat to way of life, says senator James Paterson

jacqui-lambie-tas.summary: Lambie has been a vocal critic of the Chinese government, warning in 2014 of the "unprecedented" threat from "Communist Chinese military capacity" and opposing the China-Australia Free Trade Agreement in 2015, calling the Chinese government "anti-democratic" and "a bully, thief, liar and international human rights abuser." On AUKUS, she criticised the revised submarine arrangement, telling defence officials in Senate estimates "What we should be getting is three new submarines and now we're getting trash instead."

sources.title: Capability that matters: submarine switch played down

lidia-thorpe-vic.summary: Thorpe is an outspoken advocate for Palestine. At an October 2025 pro-Palestine rally she said she would "burn down Parliament House to make a point" over conditions in Gaza (which she described as hyperbolic), and in November 2024 raised a fist and called "Free Palestine" in the Senate.

nick-mckim-tas.summary: In a Senate speech on 26 March 2025, McKim described Israel's actions in Gaza as "a genocide" and "a barbaric murder spree," said it was "utterly disgraceful how complicit Labor has allowed us to become," referencing weapons components and the Pine Gap facility, and moved a motion calling for the government to impose sanctions on "the extremist Netanyahu government," end the arms trade, and call for an end to the conflict in Gaza.

bridget-mckenzie-vic.summary: In May 2024 McKenzie criticised Senator Fatima Payman's comments on Gaza as "Absolutely appalling" after Payman accused Israel of genocide and used the phrase "From the river to the sea, Palestine will be free," and questioned what the Prime Minister would do in response.

don-farrell-sa.summary: As Trade Minister, Farrell pressed China to lift trade sanctions on Australian exports, stating "Things aren't going to get back to normal until they lift those bans"; this pressure preceded China lifting tariffs on Australian wine in April 2024. He has pursued trade diversification away from China through agreements with the UK and India.

peter-whish-wilson-tas.summary: In September 2023 Whish-Wilson joined a cross-party delegation of Australian MPs to Washington, D.C. to lobby the U.S. Department of Justice to abandon its attempts to extradite Australian publisher Julian Assange.

michelle-ananda-rajah-vic.summary: Ananda-Rajah describes herself as a supporter of Israel and the Jewish community while stating she backs Labor's position of a negotiated two-state solution. In May 2024 she said she felt "very conflicted" about the Australian government's UN vote supporting Palestinian statehood, and in July 2024 she criticised Senator Fatima Payman for crossing the floor to immediately recognise Palestine, arguing it contradicted Labor's two-state approach. She visited Israel as part of parliamentary delegations in July and December 2023.

raff-ciccone-vic.summary: Ciccone is a strong supporter of AUKUS and the Australia-US alliance, stating in an August 2025 piece that "AUKUS represents just one component of our broader defence partnership" resting on shared values and the rules-based order, and projecting it will create around 20,000 jobs. In a March 2026 Senate speech on Iran-Israel escalation he argued "Peace is not maintained by slogans or wishful thinking; it is maintained by strength, alliances and, importantly, deterrence," noted Australia's military support to the UAE, and accused the Greens of "selective outrage" and an "anti-West and anti-Australia" stance on AUKUS.

sources.title: Alliance that goes far beyond AUKUS
Middle East conflict and calling out the Greens' selective outrage

steph-hodgins-may-vic.summary: Hodgins-May is an advocate for Australia increasing its foreign aid budget and placing greater focus on international development. She previously worked in the Australian mission to the United Nations and as Greenpeace's Head of Pacific, pushing for Australia to do more to help Pacific Island nations facing climate change.

fatima-payman-wa.summary: In a May 2024 Senate speech Payman accused Israel of committing genocide against Palestinians in Gaza, criticised the Australian government's response and called for sanctions and divestment against Israel. On 25 June 2024 she crossed the floor to vote for a Greens resolution supporting Australian recognition of Palestinian statehood, after writing on 17 June 2024 that "Australia must recognise Palestine to support peace efforts"; she subsequently left the Labor Party.

jordon-steele-john-wa.summary: Steele-John, the Greens' spokesperson for Foreign Affairs and Peace & Nuclear Disarmament, has campaigned to have the government revoke the AUKUS pact and redirect funds to community services. In a November 2021 piece he called to "tear up the AUKUS deal," ratify the nuclear weapons ban treaty, ban the development and sale of weapons including autonomous weapons, close joint military facilities including Pine Gap, oppose framing China as an inevitable enemy, and pursue cooperation and diplomacy. He is co-chair of the Parliamentary Friends of the Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons.

sources.title: Greens Senator Jordon Steele-John: Charting Australia's pathway to peace

michaelia-cash-wa.summary: As Shadow Minister for Foreign Affairs, Cash affirms ANZUS and the AUKUS submarine pact as enduring alliances and describes the Five Eyes partnership and the Quad as of vital importance (May 2025). On the Middle East she takes a strongly pro-Israel position, in August 2025 calling the Albanese government's decision to "unilaterally recognise a Palestinian state ahead of a proper peace process" reckless and accusing the government of mismanaging the Australia-Israel relationship.

sources.title: Media Release - Senator Michaelia Cash Shadow Minister for Foreign Affairs
Media Release - Albanese Government Mishandles Israel Relationship

sue-lines-wa.summary: Per her Wikipedia article, Lines stated in 2019 at a WA Labor Friends of Palestine launch event that the Israel lobby is "powerful within the party and outside of the party" and influences ALP policy on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict.

glenn-sterle-wa.summary: In October 2014 Sterle used a Senate speech to condemn fellow Labor MP Melissa Parke's support for the Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions (BDS) movement, calling on her to "cease her support for divisive campaigns like the BDS movement and support a more constructive dialogue," characterising her stance as "simplistic" and "inflammatory," and reaffirming Labor policy supporting a two-state solution and lasting peace for Israel and Palestine.

sources.title: Labor senator blasts colleague over Israel

varun-ghosh-wa.summary: In his first Senate speech (25 March 2024) Ghosh stated that "our defence forces, our alliance structures and our diplomatic initiatives all play vital roles in ensuring and maintaining a rules based order in our region and around the world," and highlighted Western Australia's role as Australia's Indian Ocean capital, noting Perth is closer to Jakarta than to Canberra.

sources.title: Ghosh, Senator Varun: 25 Mar 2024: Senate debates (First Speech)
